text: The surgeon uses a trans-cystic approach to locate a single stone within the common bile duct. The stone measures nearly three centimeters, exceeding the capacity of the retrieval basket, which opens to only 1.5 centimeters.
The surgeon opens the common bile duct to perform a choledochotomy, preparing to address the large stone identified during the trans-cystic exploration.
The surgeon uses a choledochoscope to visually inspect the common bile duct, attempting to locate a large stone that did not float when flushed with saline.
The surgeon deploys a basket behind a large stone within the common bile duct and carefully drags the stone upwards, using the basket to maneuver it towards the surgical field.
The surgeon observes the stone breaking into fragments during the procedure, considering extending the choledochotomy to manage the situation.
The surgeon removes stone fragments from the common bile duct and irrigates the duct with saline solution.

After irrigating the common bile duct, the surgeon inserts the choledochoscope to visualize stone fragments that have migrated distally. The choledochoscope allows examination both proximally and distally within the duct, although the transcystic approach may limit proximal visibility depending on the cystic duct's insertion.
The surgeon uses a disposable ureteroscope to examine the distal portion of the common bile duct, ensuring that the proximal area is clear of obstructions.

The surgeon uses a grasper to remove stone fragments from the common bile duct. Afterward, the common bile duct is closed using interrupted sutures with 5-0 PDS. A completion cholangiogram is performed through the cystic duct opening to ensure no residual stones remain.
